data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: An Opportunity To Start Or Add To A Position
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc (NYSE:IIPR) is the first real estate investment trust (REIT) on the New York Stock Exchange that acquires and manages specialized properties leased to licensed operators for regulated cannabis cultivation. IIPR is a pioneer in the industry, breaking onto the scene in 2016. IIPR operates as a triple net lease REIT by providing cannabis operators capital, property, and infrastructure in exchange for rent/lease payments. Within eight years of operation, IIPR has accumulated 108 properties comprising approximately 9.0 million rentable square feet in 19 states.

IIPR has a promising future, they have a strong balance sheet, pay a juicy dividend, and have a management team of highly experienced real estate professionals. My opinion is these are all reasons to buy the stock today. However, the stock has experienced a significant drop in price in the last couple of months. This drop in price is a result of one of their largest tenants defaulting on their rent payments. I will acknowledge this default puts their short-term future at risk. However, I think this is a great opportunity to begin a new position or add to an existing one.

IIPR Balance Sheet

When considering a REIT company and if it is a good investment to purchase their stock, the balance sheet, in my opinion, is the most important financial statement available. Real estate is based on leverage, properties are not typically purchased with 100% of cash and a company or individual will use a loan to finance the purchase. When times get hard and the economy turns, having a significant amount of leverage on your balance sheet can hurt you. Understanding the amount of leverage a REIT has is valuable in understanding the health of the company. Let's take a brief look at the leverage of IIPR.

When analyzing a balance sheet, there are multiple leverage ratios to consider. One leverage ratio we will take a look at is the debt to asset ratio. The debt to asset ratio compares the total debt the company carries relative to the total assets on the balance sheet. Understanding how much debt the company has relative to their assets will help us understand how constrained IIPR's liquidity may be if times get tough and property values decrease or if the company needs to seek additional funding. According to the most recent annual report released on February 21st, 2025 IIPR has approximately $297 million in long-term debt. Compared to total assets of approximately $2.37 billion. The debt to asset ratio of IIPR is approximately 13%. Indicating, 13% of their asset value is leveraged. Indicating a very healthy debt to asset ratio.

Another leverage ratio we will look at is the debt to equity ratio. The debt to equity ratio helps can help indicate potential risks to the financial health and indicates whether a company relying on more debt or equity to finance operations. The debt to equity of IIPR is approximately 15%. Indicating IIPR is potentially relying more on equity to finance operations and investment rather than debt. However, viewing the debt to equity ratio alone does not tell the whole story. Comparing the ratio to industry peers can provide insight into how IIPR is managing their balance sheet relative to the industry. Comparing IIPR debt to equity ratios to other high profile REITs; Prologis (PLD) is 56%, Realty Income (O) is 68%, and Simon Property Group (SPG) is 824%. Comparatively speaking, IIPR has a very low debt to equity ratio. Indicating IIPR is relying less on debt to finance operations relative to their peers. Also indicating IIPR is putting themselves at less financial risk, relative to peers, when accessing the debt markets. Again indicating a healthy balance sheet and management of resources.

Dividend Payout

IIPR pays a considerable dividend with a current annual, forward, dividend rate of $7.60, purchasing shares today will yield you an approximate 10.3% yield. It is commonly preached that a high yield indicates a potentially troubled company (i.e. yield trap). Now, granted, the yield has increased substantially recently due to the sharp decline in stock price (which we will address here soon). However, the dividend is strong and is why one should consider purchasing this stock.

When considering REITs, I prefer to consider dividends relative to Funds from Operations (FFO) or Adjusted FFO (AFFO). For REITs, AFFO is the measure of the operating performance and the companies' ability to generate cash similar to free cash flow of a company. The AFFO is how much money the REIT can pay out, understanding that REITs have a requirement to pay out a percentage of its taxable income as dividends. According to the press release for the fourth quarter of 2024, IIPR paid a quarterly dividend of $1.90 and reported normalized AFFO of $2.22 - indicating IIPR had an 85% dividend payout ratio relative to the AFFO. IIPR's payout ratio is a bit higher than what I would prefer (less than 80% of FFO or AFFO) however at 85% there is still enough margin to maintain the dividend. For comparison, Prologis has a forward dividend rate of $4.04 and recent FFO of $5.56. Indicating a payout ratio of approximately 73% of FFO.

In my opinion, an 85% dividend payout ratio indicated a somewhat safe dividend. The 10% for IIPR does not indicate a dividend trap, rather, it is my opinion it indicates unfavorable sentiment and uncertainty related to the cannabis industry. In addition, IIPR has increased its dividend each year since inception (2016) almost a 10-year streak, however with an 85% payout ratio, significant near term dividend increases should not be expected.

Management Team

When it comes to Real Estate Investing, I frequent the management team page to understand who is running the company. For IIPR, the C-Suite and even the board comprises very experienced real estate professionals. Here I want to highlight a few members of the management team and why having them lead the company should let you sleep well at night.

The Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Paul Smither, has served as the CEO since the formation of IIPR. Paul has previous experience working for an aerospace manufacturer and, prior to working in manufacturing, worked as a lawyer in the commercial and real estate transaction space. The Chief Operating Officer (COO) of the organization, Catherine Hastings, has been at the company since 2017. She has held many roles internally, including Chief Financial Officer, treasurer, and Chief Accounting Officer. Prior to her time here at IIPR, Catherine was a Vice President at BioMed Realty Trust, Inc. a REIT specializing in acquiring, leasing, developing, and managing laboratory and office space for the life science industry. Another heavily regulated, niche, real estate industry.

Last but not least, the anchor of the management team; Alan Gold. Alan currently serves as the Executive Chairman and frankly, IIPR could not have a better Executive Chairman to lead this board. Alan has decades of real estate experience. Previously, Alan was Executive Chairman of IQHQ, Inc (IQHQ); Chairman, President; CEO of BioMed Realty Trust, Inc. (BioMed); and Co-Founder and President of Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc (ARE). The companies, IQHQ, BioMed, and ARE all operate in the life sciences industry, an industry known to have high regulations and very specialized properties, indicating Alan has had extensive experience being a manager in a high regulated and specialized real estate industry.

The management team is highly experienced and very capable of navigating the murky waters of the cannabis industry. In my opinion, all faith can be placed into the team to lead IIPR through tough times and good times.

Recent News Leads to Significant Price Drop

On December 20, 2024, IIPR reported defaults by PharmaCann Inc. (PharmaCann) on six of 11 leases. The default occurred during the month of December and totals $4.2 million in rent, property management fees, taxes, and insurance payments. Following this news, IIPR shares gapped down about 22% as the market digested the news. The default by PharmaCann is significant, understanding the company represents approximately 17% of IIPR's total rental revenues.

About a month after the news broke, IIPR reported a resolution to defaults by PharmaCann. The resolution generally included the following:

  • IIPR applies security deposits held under all leases for the payment of defaulted rents in December 2024 and January 2025;
  • Existing leases at nine of the 11 properties were amended, increasing total required security deposits and reducing base rent by 7%;
  • Select properties will be transitioned to new tenants by August 1, 2025;
  • Additional equity capital contributed to PharmaCann and issuance of a secured promissory note from PharmaCann to IIPR; and
  • PharmaCann is not allowed to incur additional indebtedness without IIPR's consent.

The rapid response to the defaults and the in-depth resolution established by IIPR represents a masterclass in tenant management. I believe this exemplifies the leadership abilities of the management. However, given the additional equity capital and promissory note, IIPR is further rooting their relationship with PharmaCann their largest tenant. This may prove to be costly if PharmaCann is not able to improve their business.

An Opportunity to Purchase a High Yielder

Even though the default by PharmaCann indicates the fragile nature of the cannabis industry, it does not take away from the overall health and strength of IIPR as a REIT. IIPR has multiple tenants, and PharmaCann only represents 16.3% of the annualized base rent. They have a diverse base of tenants in multiple different states. Additionally, IIPR is not in the cannabis industry directly, they simply provide land and infrastructure to operators. The cannabis industry is mature enough, there will always be operators looking to lease space and buildings.

IIPR's leadership has exemplified their ability to maintain a strong balance sheet and navigate difficult situations while providing exceptional shareholder returns. With IIPR's balance sheet, I do not expect a dividend cut to occur due to PharmaCann's defaults. In their most recent quarterly report on February 19th, there was no mention of a dividend cut.

Buying IIPR right now gives you a solid 10% yield that, I believe, will continue to be increased in the future. Additionally, the stock is at 52-week lows and, barring any other news of tenant rent defaults, I would not expect to go any lower. However, the investor should expect to experience more volatility in the stock price until the cannabis industry receives more clear guidance from the federal government.

data.title: Compass Pathways: Betting On The Future Of Psilocybin Therapy
data.description:
Skepticism, regulatory hurdles, and high cash burn have characterized the psychedelic biotech sector in the recent past. Most investors have thrown COMPASS Pathways plc (NASDAQ:CMPS) into the same ailing basket, betting that its psilocybin-based treatment will meet the fate of other speculative biotech plays. But behind the noise, I believe Compass is positioning itself to take advantage of regulatory tailwinds, compelling clinical data, and a financial cushion that goes well beyond its upcoming catalysts. But even as broader sentiment remains bearish in the space, this mispricing presents a window into opportunities for investors willing to look beyond short-term volatility.

Latest Financials

Compass Pathways reported a net loss of $155.1 million for 2024 compared to a $118.5 million loss in 2023. That's in line with its late-stage clinical progress, as R&D spending jumped to $119 million from $87.5 million last year. The company clearly is funneling capital into its Phase 3 trials for COMP360, which often squeeze cash reserves but are necessary for any biotech moving toward commercialization. General and administrative expenses also grew to $59.2 million due to increased headcount and legal costs as the company positioned itself better for regulatory milestones and possible partnerships. Of course, rising costs are not unheard of in pre-revenue biotech firms, but the question is whether Compass has the financial runway to keep its ambitions without a dilutive raise in the near term.

End of 2024, cash reserves were $165.1 million, down from $220.2 million a year earlier. Given an estimated cash burn of $120 million to $145 million by 2025, Compass has about 4 to 6 quarters of runway before needing another capital infusion. But the company raised another $140.4 million in early 2025, giving it time to deliver data readouts that could change its valuation narrative. If its next Phase 3 results show strong efficacy, Compass might be able to raise non-dilutive funding in partnership rather than issuing new equity at low prices. Such a financial cushion suggests the company is not in immediate distress, but I would watch for changes in spending that accelerate cash depletion.

Psychedelic Neuroscience

Compass is targeting the two most challenging psychiatric conditions: Treatment-resistant depression and post-traumatic stress disorder, both of which have seen little progress over time in terms of viable approaches. Most traditional antidepressants such as SSRIs and SNRIs take weeks or longer before providing meaningful relief. The mechanism behind COMP360, Compass's proprietary synthetic psilocybin formulation, is quite different. Unlike conventional treatments that increase serotonin availability over time, psilocybin produces a rapid, transient psychedelic state that "resets" neural networks associated with rigid, maladaptive thought patterns. This is mediated mainly by 5-HT2A receptor agonism and results in increased neuroplasticity and temporary dismantling of the default mode network, a brain system involved in self-referential thinking and rumination - core issues in depression and PTSD.

The uniqueness of COMP360 is that it can provide therapeutic benefits with a single administration, unlike the daily dosing of most antidepressants. A one-time 25 mg dose in the PTSD trial resulted in significant reductions in Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale scores, with 77.3% maintaining response at 12 weeks. Such data suggests a durable effect that could make psilocybin-based therapies economically more attractive than chronic regimens. As opposed to other psychedelic-focused biotech firms pursuing ketamine- or MDMA-assisted therapy, Compass is betting that psilocybin can provide long-term symptom relief without the addiction risks of other compounds. Compass has the largest study of psilocybin ever conducted and is positioned to be the most clinically rigorous player in the space, potentially conferring a regulatory and commercial advantage.

COMP360’s Clinical Trajectory

Compass Pathways enters a pivotal period with the introduction of its COMP360 psilocybin therapy into late-stage trials. The company's Phase 3 program in treatment-resistant depression is the largest randomized, controlled psilocybin study to date, with key data readouts likely to shape the company and the wider psychedelic sector going forward. The first major catalyst is the six-week topline data from the COMP005 trial arriving in the second quarter of 2025, which is the primary endpoint for regulatory consideration. If results support the endurance of response observed in early trials, Compass will have an important edge in its push for FDA approval. A second large readout from COMP006 in 2026 will provide 26-week data - an important marker of long-term efficacy. Although the rapid-acting antidepressant effects of psilocybin are well documented, regulators will want to see sustained improvement before full approval.

Outside of TRD, Compass is also quietly preparing for COMP360's entry into the PTSD market, which has an even greater unmet need and no new FDA-approved treatments in more than two decades. The company's Phase 2 trial met safety and efficacy endpoints, with 63.6% of participants in remission by week four. Such findings from a small open-label study suggest that COMP360 could be generalizable to other psychiatric conditions driven by rigid, trauma-related neural patterns. With additional capital secured in early 2025, Compass can accelerate a late-stage PTSD program without losing focus on trauma recovery. The next year will determine if COMP360 becomes a legitimate breakthrough in psychiatric treatment, with regulatory clarity likely determining its trajectory in the short and long term.

Regulatory Hurdles and Scientific Doubts

COMP360 presents significant risks that investors should not ignore. The regulatory landscape for psychedelic-based treatments remains murky even with the FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation. Even though Compass has taken among the most structured clinical approaches in the sector, the agency may require longer-term safety and efficacy data before full approval. Psychedelics remain politically and culturally contentious and resistance from more conservative regulatory bodies may delay commercialization. Even if COMP360 is approved, there is no guarantee that insurers will offer reimbursement, given the therapy's unique administration model that requires intense psychological support. As opposed to standard antidepressants that can be prescribed and refilled easily with minimal oversight, psilocybin-assisted therapy involves prolonged in-clinic sessions that may limit scalability and complicate widespread adoption.

Scientists remain skeptical about psilocybin's long-term efficacy. While the Phase 2 Compass results in TRD and PTSD are promising, some academics argue that the observed improvements are at least partially related to increased suggestibility due to the increased neurobiological effect of psychedelic experiences. It is notoriously difficult to control for the placebo effect in psychedelic studies, since patients know immediately whether they have received an active dose. Other questions are related to treatment response durability. Although chronic conditions such as depression and PTSD warrant sustained symptom reduction over 12 weeks according to early data with COMP360, the need for repeat dosing is still debatable. If COMP360 ultimately requires several treatment sessions per year, its economic viability and practical feasibility might be challenged, particularly in healthcare systems unwilling to accept new psychiatric interventions. However, advocates point out that the rapid action of psilocybin therapy and its capacity to induce neuroplasticity suggest a fundamentally new paradigm in psychiatric care that may be superior to the trial-and-error of conventional antidepressants. Whether these scientific and regulatory hurdles can be overcome by Compass will decide whether it becomes a niche player or a dominant force in mental health treatment.

Current Valuation

Traditional valuation metrics are difficult to apply to pre-revenue biotech firms like Compass because earnings have yet to materialize. However, the company has a 1.39 price-to-book ratio - significantly below the sector median of 2.12 - which suggests the market is undervaluing its asset base relative to its peers.

In terms of Compass's potential, we can look at the market for its lead indication, treatment-resistant depression. MDD impacts about 16.1 million adults annually in the United States. About 30% of these - or roughly 4.8 million people - are TRD-positive. TRD has a huge economic burden - with $43.8 billion annually in healthcare costs, lost productivity, and unemployment. With regulatory approval, COMP360 could drive significant revenue from only a small slice of the total market.

Takeaways

In its current state, Compass is navigating a complex landscape where scientific promise, regulatory uncertainty, and financial pressures intersect. The company’s commitment to rigorous clinical trials sets it apart from many players in the psychedelic space, positioning COMP360 as the most credible psilocybin-based therapy in development. Its financial runway, bolstered by a recent capital raise, provides enough flexibility to reach critical data readouts, but long-term sustainability will depend on regulatory approvals and commercial adoption. While skepticism remains about the scalability and durability of psilocybin-assisted therapy, early clinical results suggest a meaningful and potentially lasting impact on treatment-resistant depression and PTSD.

If Compass successfully transitions from an experimental biotech to a commercial-stage company, I believe the current market valuation could prove to be deeply discounted relative to its long-term potential. Investors willing to tolerate near-term volatility and regulatory unpredictability may find an asymmetric risk-reward opportunity, as the company moves toward pivotal milestones in the near to mid future.

data.title: Nvidia: Expect Growth Momentum To Continue With The Rise Of Agentic AI
data.description:
In my earlier coverage of Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA), I mentioned that the fourth quarter earnings report may provide solid confirmation of expected growth and serve as a catalyst for moving towards the world's largest stock by market capitalization. While the market does not seem to be too excited about the Q4 earnings report released on Feb. 26, I am finding it very encouraging and feel more confident about my long-term secular growth thesis for NVDA. I will share my insights below and reiterate my strong buy rating on NVDA, believing NVDA remains the best idea for secular growth investment in the next 5 years due to the coming waves of AI Agents and Robotics AI.

Nvidia Key Update

Nvidia just reported very solid Q4 (2024) earnings. The report indicates that the revenue is up 78% year-over-year, reaching $39.3 billion, and the net income posts an 80% year-over-year increase with $22.1 billion.

The company's data center revenue was $35.6 billion, accounting for 90% of sales and a 93% increase from last year. As shown in a separate report, Nvidia's GPU market share continues to increase at 90%, while its main competitor, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), is in danger of dropping to a single digit in market share. AMD's data center GPUs are growing at only 69%.

The AI is the main driving force for the growth. The following is a summary of the latest developments in Nvidia's main leading AI products with Blackwell.

  • Blackwell is very performant and is scaling very well. It will be “incredible across the board” according to the Q1 conference call. Nvidia indicated that AI models for reasoning “were tens of times faster, 25 times higher throughput.” I expect Blackwell to play a significant role in the coming waves of AI agents. I will share more details on the Agentic AI later.
  • NVDA is fully recovered from the previous hiccup that seems to cost two months per Nvidia's conference call. I believe that the recovery would put any lingering concerns over the hiccup to rest.
  • The Blackwell is in full swing production. Q4 saw the fastest product ramp in the company's history. The management team expects the strong momentum to continue in Q1 2025.
  • The Next transition to Blackwell Ultra is expected to be much smoother compared to the transition from Harper. Blackwell “will slot right in.”

However, some analysts and investors may expect Nvidia's earnings report to bring “hotter” growth data and express concerns about the gross profit margin for Q4, which was 73% compared to 75% in the previous quarter. It is expected to be lower in the coming first quarter (about 71%) as mentioned in the Q1 earnings call. In what follows, I will present my views on the growth prospects of NVDA and share some insights into these concerning areas.

Growth Insight 1: New growth market from the rise of Agentic AI

According to Gartner, “Intelligent agents in AI are nascent but quickly maturing.” These AI agents will make AI more useful. 2025 is expected to mark “the rise of AI agents” as called by Forbes.

I did some research using Google Trends with AI agents and Agentic AI. As shown below, we can see that there is a sharp jump in the daily interest from a few to 100 in a short time of 6 months.

In the world of AI computing, a big part of the Agentic AI is the reasoning process, where the reasoning AI models will demand much more computing power than before with ChatGPT in the past two years. Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang indicated that reasoning is expected to be “100 times more than what we used to do.”

The following is my take on the computing power involved with the Agentic AI, following Jensen's thoughts in the conference calls:

  • Pre-training is to gain basic (human) knowledge. It is an essential first step. ChatGPT could be used to estimate the bulk of the computing power required. The growth has been reflected in the fast build-up of the AI data center for the past two years or so.
  • Post training with feedback, including reinforcement learning with human feedback. This is a process that may involve many computing-intensive operations, such as creating synthetic data, testing, experimenting, and iterating (repeating) learning steps. Notice post training is now more widely deployed with many models, including ChatGPT, Grok, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), etc. We can see that the need for computing power multiples fast here.
  • Reasoning process can be viewed as an advanced inference. It involves AI making the choice (decision) on a few options from the post-training for the best fitting solution. Note that the combination of all these will be exponentially “sky-high,” leading to Jensen's “100X” compute demand.

The key takeaway is that the computing need for implementing Agentic AI could be hundreds of times more than we have experienced. This is where NVDA's GPUs stand to reap the most benefits in the upcoming years, attributed to its leapfrog leading advantage established with Blackwell, including the reasoning ability mentioned earlier.

Growth Insight 2: Robotic AI will shape up the computing upgrade for traditional industries

Robotic AI is about the creation of intelligent machines for performing certain physical tasks. Some of Nvidia's robotic demos are good examples. The key growth opportunities lie in many traditional industries where the computing power is still far lagging from the “high” tech industries like mobile phones, notebooks, PCs, data centers, cloud computing, etc. For example, high-tech EVs will have more advanced computing like GPUs in them, while the microchips in the gas cars require much less computing power. The computing upgrade is very slow in those traditional industries such as manufacturing, warehousing, etc. With the advent of robotic AI, industries adopting this technology will become what I call “computational addiction,” just like how people upgrade their iPhones every two years and businesses upgrade their PCs every three years. Robots not only require computing power to meet the AI computing capabilities during the creation process, but also require continuous computing upgrades. This is one of the reasons why Jensen Huang predicted that computing power may grow a “millionfold” in 10 years.

Huang indicated that robotic AI (“physical AI” in his words) opportunities have not been included in the company's forecast for 2025. I believe that in the future, large-scale deployment of robotic (physical) AI will become more pervasive, and it offers a good long-term theme for investing in NVDA stocks. On the other hand, Nvidia appears to be ready to explore this type of scaling growth opportunities. For example, the company indicated in the Q4 conference call that:

“Grace Blackwell NVLink 72 rack is an engineering marvel. 1.5 million components produced across 350 manufacturing sites by nearly 100,000 factory operators.”

Margin will recover to the middle 70s

One of the key operational questions is NVDA's gross margin, which was around 75% before dropping to 73% in Q4. Nvidia also officially lowered the margin target of Q1 to a range that includes below 70% at the lower end. However, the management mentioned in the conference call that they are confident to bring the margin to mid-70s later in 2025 once the ramping up of the Backwell manufacturing is completed. This decline is temporary and is expected to occur during the transition period for Blackwell.

Notice that after Q4 earnings, the current PEG (forward) ratio is at a deeper discount (-54.72%) compared to the sector median, as shown below:

It may represent the lowered expectation of gross margin and the skepticism about the future growth.

Cheap valuation for a secular growth stock

If NVDA could keep the gross margin at 70% or higher, the estimates below should make NVDA one of the cheapest growth stocks in 2027 when P/E ratio will be 23.55 with earnings growth at 25.37%. The estimates for 2026 also appear to be cheap with P/E below 30% considering a 48% EPS growth. This might explain why PEG is at -54.72%. So NVDA should be considered very cheap based on the comparison with the sector median. Notice that PEG value is also very cheap at a -58% discount when compared to the 5Y average as shown in the table above.

Revenue (Sales) growth is projected at 50% for 2026, which will improve the price-to-sales (P/S) ratio. The 21% revenue growth for 2027 is high but appears to be conservative based on the previous discussion of growth opportunities.

Keep in mind, Nvidia has guided the numbers up for the next quarter. So I expect the stock to recover significantly from the current price level (around $120) once the current market correction (downtrend) is completed. My price target for NVDA is $220 in the next 12 months. It is calculated based on the assumption of recovering 80% from the -54% PEG discount.

Risks and Caveats

Agent AI and physical AI are still in their early stages. Any long-term forecast of NVDA GPU demand growth requires further conclusive evidence. Due to the expected lower GDP in the fourth quarter of 2024 compared to the previous quarter, the trend of the US economy slowing down in the short term is becoming increasingly evident. If the macroeconomy continues to slow down further, AI capital expenditures will decrease, significantly impacting NVDA's sales.

The impact of tariffs on hardware products is often more tangible than on software products or services. NVDA has so far shown solid growth without the help of the Chinese market. However, it is uncertain how much impact the implementation of the new tariffs will have. Similarly, stricter chip sanctions may affect the sales of low-end GPUs to China.

The risk of GPU competition does not seem to be a factor, as AMD's market share in GPUs has been declining to possibly single digits. However, in the new fields such as AI agents and inference models, the alternative computing solutions from AMD and Broadcom may still pose a threat to NVDA's long-term growth.

Closing Thoughts

NVDA remains my best idea for the secular growth over the next 5–10 years due to the new AI growth catalysts such as the rise of Agentic AI and the start of Robotic (physical) AI. The stock price appears to be cheap as the growth has been assured by the Q4 earnings, including the estimates for Q1 (2025) that still show solid growth driven by the strong demand for Blackwell. The recent lack of excitement about NVDA may stem from the short-term growth scares toward mega-cap stocks in the market. The current sentiment is quite negative as the market is going through a correction. However, the long-term investors may find decent opportunities to add more NVDA shares at the current price level.

data.title: OneSpan Guides For A Flat FY25 And Expectations Weigh -- Stock Not Attractive
data.description:
OneSpan Inc.'s (NASDAQ:OSPN) share price has declined since January alongside the broader tech sector, potentially even harder due to elevated expectations.

The company’s Q4 2024 earnings results, released this week, did little to change the narrative, as management guided for flattish revenue and EBITDA in 2025.

With OSPN trading at close to 15x NTM earnings and growth challenges persisting, the stock seems fairly valued but does not present an impressive opportunity. I maintain my Hold rating.

Q4 2024 results

Cost cuts moving to cost expansion: OneSpan delivered its strongest quarter yet in terms of profitability, with Q4 adjusted EBITDA reaching $20M and full-year 2024 adjusted EBITDA at $74M. These gains came as a result of the company’s impressive cost-cutting initiatives.

However, management has repeatedly stated that further margin expansion through cost reductions will be difficult, and the company is shifting its focus toward fueling revenue growth with higher investment (i.e., higher costs).

Growth is hard: While the company highlights several growing metrics like ARR (+8.5%), subscription revenue (+30%), and software revenue (+16%)—these have come at the expense of other declining areas such as hardware (-23%) and on-premise software maintenance. Even within the software, the Digital Agreements segment (+20%) is growing in part at the expense of Security Solutions (-1%) because of a transfer of products from SS to DA.

More importantly, management has not discussed any significant new growth-generating activities for several quarters.

On product, the company continues to comment on FIDO2 hardware authentication, which has been mentioned multiple times but has yet to generate meaningful impact. A new CTO was recently hired, which may signal future product expansion, but no details were provided on the call.

On the channel or marketing side, OneSpan has long discussed leveraging channel partners to drive growth. However, management has provided no meaningful updates on this initiative in 4Q24 either.

Management has confirmed that growth is coming and will continue to come from existing customers and not new logos.

As a result, I continue to believe OneSpan’s long-term growth rate is likely to be in the MSD range, barring a meaningful catalyst from product innovation or new customer acquisition.

Flat guidance for FY25: Management guided for flat revenue and adjusted EBITDA in 2025. Full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance is $72–$76M, which is below Q4’s run rate of $80M. This suggests limited near-term upside, as margin expansion has largely played out and revenue growth remains modest.

On the topline side, the company is expected to grow 1/3%, barely above inflation, with potentially 1/2% of headwind coming from FX. Management says that this includes headwinds from hardware sales declining (a secular trend), but this is also a tailwind for software, cloud, etc.

Valuation: more reasonable, but not attractive

OSPN is down ~20% since the recent Nasdaq peak in January.

Based on 2025 guidance for $75M in adjusted EBITDA, subtracting $9M in D&A, $9M in stock-based compensation (SBC), and assuming a 25% tax rate, I estimate net income of ~$43M.

Compared with a market cap of $620 million, this comes as a 14.4x P/E. This implies a 7% earnings yield, which is better than the 6.5% calculated in 3Q24 (based on the more profitable Digital Agreements segment), but still not particularly attractive for a company that is growing at MSD rate, not significantly expanding its customer base, and not making major moves into new channels.

OneSpan Inc. has executed well on cost-cutting, but with the company now entering a flatter growth phase, it is difficult to justify a 15x (much less the January 20x) earnings multiple. I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: Waste Management Could See Further Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Waste Management, Inc. (NYSE:WM) should continue benefiting from a strong pricing environment. In addition, volume recovery in the industrial end market coupled with easing comparisons in residential should help revenue growth. Further, the company's entry into the medical waste market through the Stericycle acquisition as well as its investment in renewable natural gas ('RNG') business should drive long-term growth.

The company's margin outlook is also positive with strong pricing, mix improvement, cost synergies from integration of the recent Stericycle acquisition, and management's cost reduction initiatives helping margins. The stock is trading at a discount to its peers, and I see a reasonable upside moving forward. Hence, I rate the stock a buy.

Waste Management’s Revenue Analysis and Outlook

I last covered Waste Management with a bullish rating in December when I talked about the company’s good growth prospects helped by price increases and volume recovery.

My thesis proved correct, and the company has reported good Q4 results and the stock is up ~8.23% since then, nicely outperforming S&P 500’s flattish performance.

In the fourth quarter of FY24, revenue benefitted from continued price increases and increases in recycled commodity prices, along with Stericycle (now WM Healthcare Solutions) acquisition. As a result, revenue grew by 13% Y/Y to $5.893 bn. Excluding the Stericycle acquisition, the legacy business saw 5.2% Y/Y growth.

On a segment basis, the Collection and Disposal segment’s revenue increased by 4.3% Y/Y. The Recycling Processing and Sales segment’s revenue increased by 14% Y/Y. Lastly, the WM Renewable Energy segment’s revenue increased by 24% Y/Y.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company’s growth prospects driven by good pricing discipline, improving volume growth outlook and the benefits from the company’s strategic investments in growth markets like sustainability and healthcare.

The companies operating in the waste management industry have a good moat as the landfill resources they own are scarce, and it is not easy to open new landfills closer to populated areas - where waste is generated - as there is opposition from the local population. This gives companies good pricing power. Waste Management has seen strong pricing power in recent years and has been able to implement healthy pricing increases in response to rising inflation. Management expects this pricing momentum to continue in 2025 and expects a 5.8% to 6.2% Y/Y core pricing increase and a 4% to 4.2% yield improvement Y/Y.

On the volume front, the company has seen some headwinds in the industrial end market as high interest rates were impacting industrial production, and the company was also shedding some low-margin residential volumes. However, these headwinds are largely behind us. The industrial volumes should start picking up as the interest cycle reversing should result in improvement in Industrial production moving forward. In addition, the recent reshoring trends and the Trump Administration’s focus on encouraging domestic manufacturing is also a long-term positive for this market. On the residential side, the company has seen some headwinds from the exit from low margin volumes last year. As the company laps these headwinds in FY25, it should result in easier comparisons, helping volume growth.

Management has guided for 0.25% to 0.75% volume growth for 2025 and this guidance doesn’t include any meaningful rebound in the Industrial market. I am a bit more positive regarding the industrial end market, given President Trump’s past track record and his publicly stated stance regarding supporting U.S. manufacturing. So, I see a potential upside to the volume guidance.

In addition to good pricing and end market growth outlook, the company should also benefit from its investments in renewable and recycling markets, as well as entry into the high-growth medical waste market through Stericycle acquisition.

The company has bought five new renewable natural gas (RNG) plants online in 2024 and plans to commission eight new RNG plants in 2025. As the company continues to open new plants and ramp up production at existing plants, I expect this to be a good growth driver for the company in the medium term. The company is also locking long-term contracts to reduce its exposure to market volatility and for 2025, almost 50% of its RNG output is already locked at favorable prices. For the recycling business also, the company is transitioning to a “fee-for-service” model, derisking revenue and margins from commodity price volatility. For FY2024, the company’s recycling and renewable energy businesses contributed ~$363 mm in EBITDA. Management expects $190 mm in incremental EBITDA contribution from growth investments this fiscal year and expects operating EBITDA from these two segments to approach $800 mm by 2027. This should add nicely to the strong performance of the company’s core Collection and Disposal business.

Another growth market for the company is Healthcare/ Medical Waste. The company entered this market recently with the acquisition of Stericycle. Given the aging U.S. population, this market is expected to grow at a higher pace compared to the company’s traditional Waste Collection business. In addition, management also sees cross-sell synergies by leveraging its existing customer base.

Overall, the company’s revenue outlook is positive with pricing and volume outlook remaining favorable in its traditional waste collection business with good medium to long-term growth potential in renewable, recycling, and medical waste markets.

Waste Management’s Margin Analysis and Outlook

In the fourth quarter of FY24, the adjusted EBITDA margins declined by 100 bps Y/Y due to higher commodity pricing in the recycling brokerage business, higher incentive compensation and employee healthcare costs, and integration costs from the Stericycle acquisition, which was partially offset by better pricing, cost optimization from automation and better mix as lower margin volumes in residential collection business were dropped.

On a segment basis, the Collection and Disposal segment’s adjusted EBITDA margins improved by 40 bps Y/Y. The Recycling Processing and Sales segment’s adjusted EBITDA margins declined by 60 bps Y/Y. Lastly, the WM Renewable Energy segment’s adjusted EBITDA margins declined by 280 bps Y/Y.

Moving forward, the company’s margin outlook is positive. The company’s strong pricing growth should help it more than offset inflationary headwinds, helping margins. The mix should also be positive in the core business, given the company’s efforts to exit some low-margin residential contracts. In addition, the company should also benefit from cost synergies from the integration of the recent Stericycle acquisition.

The company is also investing in automation to offset rising labor cost inflation. Management has given a target of $450 mm in cost-saving help by automation and related job cuts. I believe these significant cost savings coupled with the price increase should help the company improve margins moving forward.

Valuations and Rating

WM stock is trading at 29.84x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $7.66 and 26.28x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $8.70. This is a discount versus its peers Republic Services, Inc. (RSG) trading at 33.70x FY25 and 30.56x FY26 consensus EPS estimates and Waste Connections, Inc. (WCN) trading at 35.59x FY25 and 31.07x FY26 consensus estimates.

Management guidance and consensus estimates also look conservative, as they are not building in any meaningful recovery in the Industrial end market. So, there is a chance for upward revision in EPS estimates.

Over the last 5-years, the stock has traded at an average forward P/E of 28.62x. Given the company is taking steps to reduce volatility and provide better visibility/ predictability in its RNG and waste recycling business through longer-term contracts, a case can be made for some re-rating in valuation multiple in line with its peers. However, even if we assume the stock trades at its 5-year historical average P/E of 28.62x, using FY26 consensus EPS estimates, we get a one-year forward price target of $248.99 (= 28.62 x consensus FY26 EPS estimate of $8.70).

This implies a ~8.88% upside from yesterday's closing price of $228.69. Add to that ~1.44% dividend yield, the total return can be in the low double-digit percentage, which is reasonable given the low risk/essential nature of the business. There is further upside potential if EPS estimates get revised upwards or the valuation multiple re-rates in line with its peers. So, I continue to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

Risks

  • The company has recently acquired Stericycle, and I am expecting good revenue and cost synergies from it. However, if the integration of this acquisition doesn’t go as expected, the stock may react negatively.

  • Also, I expect the Industrial waste market to improve moving forward. However, if the Industrial/ Manufacturing sector sees a slowdown for some reason, the company’s revenues may be negatively impacted.

Takeaway

Waste Management's revenue should benefit from healthy pricing, volume recovery, investments in growing RNG and recycling business, and entry into the medical waste market through Stericycle acquisition. Its margins outlook is also positive given strong pricing and management’s cost reduction plan. The stock is trading at a discount to its peers and offers reasonable upside with low risk.

data.title: Zeta Global Q4: Beaten Down But Not Broken
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (NYSE:ZETA) delivered an earnings report that was set to demolish the bear arguments holding back its stock. To that effect, Zeta came out and offered investors strong guidance for its revenue growth rates to continue moving higher at 20% CAGR over the next 3 years.

And what was the investors' reaction? Something in the range between apathetic and unenthusiastic, with the stock moving lower post earnings.

For my part, after careful consideration, I believe that even when we consider the overall weakness in the market, where tons of really compelling opportunities are available right now, ZETA still appears to be above average, at 22x forward free cash flow.

Rapid Recap -- Stupendous Bad Call!

No sooner did I make a bullish call on Zeta, than the stock dropped 50%! Man, that was a terrible call.

And I get it. Given this really bad call, what makes me believe that now is a good time for Zeta? Allow me to explain.

Zeta's Near-Term Prospects

Zeta Global is a marketing technology company that helps businesses reach customers more effectively. This is, basically, a smaller version of Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) meets HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS).

Like Salesforce, it helps big companies manage customer relationships, and like HubSpot, it streamlines marketing efforts.

But Zeta Global strives to go further by using AI to analyze data to predict what people are interested in. It then personalizes its customer marketing campaigns—think of it like a super-smart marketing assistant that knows what works and then automates workflows.

The idea is that Zeta Global boosts sales by delivering ads and messages that resonate positively with customers and could move its revenue line to over $2 billion by 2028.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Zeta Provides Significant Visibility

Zeta came out with bold guidance. Not only has it confidently guided for this year to be up around 26% y/y, but it's even gone as far as to provide its guidance for the next 3 years.

On the surface, this looks rather unappetizing, given that Zeta is basically stating that its high-growth-rate days are now in the rearview mirror.

And as an investor, that's never a great setup when the outlook ahead points to a deceleration in the company's growth rates! Not good.

Plus, we have to keep track of the fact that Q1 2025 will be the easiest comparable for the next several quarters.

Furthermore, with each passing quarter, the comparables will get tougher and tougher and tougher, so that perhaps by H2 2025, its revenue growth rates will have gone from growing at 50% y/y in Q4 2024 all the way down to sub 25% CAGR. That level of deceleration is a tough pill to swallow!

And yet, I wonder, how much of that has already been priced in?

ZETA Stock Valuation -- 22x Forward Free Cash Flow

Zeta holds about 4% net cash relative to its market cap. It's not the highest metric around, and given the recent dip in the market, one can afford to be a little more picky than normal. As a reference point, I generally like to recommend stocks that have 5% net cash to market cap.

Moving on, Zeta's free cash flow margin for 2025 is pointing to around 11%. Even if we allow room for Zeta to be conservative with its guidance, and its free cash flow margins to reach 14% this year, we are still looking at about $200 million of free cash flow in the best case. That's a tough ask, but not wholly out of the realm of possibilities.

If that transpires to play out, this would put Zeta priced at about 22x forward free cash flow, a figure that, I believe, is completely justified.

Moreover, given that Zeta has come out and put out 3 years guidance for its free cash flow margins to reach at least 16% by 2028, this will further support this year's bull case.

In essence, you have a well-positioned growth company that can provide investors with a lot of visibility. I believe that this could, in time, become an institutional-favorite holding.

Risk Factors to Consider

Undoubtedly, the main risk factor that I'm thinking about is that Zeta's revenue growth rates are decelerating rapidly. We've just crossed $1 billion in revenues, and the growth rates are slowing.

How much confidence can I really put in its 2028 guidance to reach $2.2 billion in revenues?

Luckily, I don't believe we need to know that answer, since paying 22x forward free cash flow already forgives a lot of those difficult-to-answer questions.

Another risk factor is that Zeta has to convince companies to switch from their existing marketing platforms. Case in point, many big brands already use tools from Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Salesforce, which are major competitors for Zeta in this space, and no pushovers.

The Bottom Line

At the end of the day, I believe Zeta Global is a compelling growth story.

Yes, there's been a sell-off and concerns about deceleration.

And sure, the road ahead won’t be smooth, but with solid free cash flow guidance and long-term visibility, I see more upside than downside.

I’m not ignoring the risks, but at 22x forward free cash flow and growth of around 25% this year, I think the market is being overly cautious.

Time will tell, but I’m bullish on Zeta.

data.title: A February Fumble: S&P 500 Falls, Trump Asserts His Presence
data.description:
Stocks stumbled in February after rallying to begin the year. The SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) fell by more than 3% for the month as tariff fears, renewed inflation worries, a dip in expected GDP, and a plunge among momentum stocks all weighed.

Selling pressure was sharp among large-cap US tech, as evidenced by the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) giving more than 4%. Tesla (TSLA) cratered, nearing a 50% haircut from its December high, making it the worst of the Magnificent Seven drawdowns. It was bloody among small caps, too; the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) lost almost 7% despite a fall in Treasury yields.

It wasn’t all bad. International developed market equities enjoyed a 2% advance, while the emerging markets tallied a fractional gain. It was the second-best month for the Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) versus the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) in the past 15 years. Weakness among the Mag 7 and a lower US Dollar Index (DXY) help foreign stocks post alpha.

In the bond market, Treasurys tallied their best month since last July. The yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note fell by more than 30 basis points in February, but not because of a more sanguine inflation view. Rather, growth worries permeated asset classes as DOGE makes its mark on federal employment levels. Consumer spending also shows some signs of stalling after a strong Q4.

Stocks Fell Over the Second Half of February, Bonds Rallied

Turning to the sectors, it actually wasn’t all that bad outside of Consumer Discretionary (XLY), which was, of course, weighed down by a nearly 30% monthly plunge in Tesla. Consumer Staples (XLP), Real Estate (XLRE), and Energy (XLE) were all solidly in the green, while Information Technology (XLK) and Industrials were down materially. Capping a soft month for tech, Nvidia (NVDA) shares plunged by more than 8% after it reported earnings last Wednesday night.

The safety-trade was on for much of the back half of February. What’s more, despite a 3% drop in oil prices, the Energy sector was up. Real Estate hovered just under its YTD high by late Friday as the beleaguered property sector catches a solid bid to begin 2025.

February Sector Returns: Mixed Performances, Discretionary Down Hard

As for the economy, the rearview mirror looks just fine. It’s the outlook that’s cloudy. Early in the month, the January jobs report revealed another drop in the unemployment rate despite a sequential decline in monthly job growth. Average hourly earnings were in check in what was generally a Goldilocks report. Markets failed to rally, however, and that set the tone for the balance of the month.

This past Thursday, a surprising spike in Initial Claims brought labor-market fears into focus. DC-area layoffs spiked, underscoring Elon Musk’s DOGE’s efforts to trim the public workforce. Given tighter immigration policy likely over the years ahead, I expect negative monthly payroll prints to be a real possibility. The breakeven employment increase level is likely under 100k monthly. Finally, many states have triggered the Sahm Rule, and we’ll see if that measure gets hit again at the national level.

A Steady Labor Market

The consumer drove much of US GDP growth over recent months, but the Census Bureau reported that household spending took a notable dip to begin 2025. At –0.9% sequentially, it was the worst headline Retail Sales print since March 2023.

Still, weekly Johnson Redbook Retail Sales shows a steady consumer, with 5-6% annual growth. Last Friday’s January Personal Spending data backed up what the Census Bureau put out mid-month, revealing a weak –0.2% month-on-month change.

US Retail Sales Stumbles In January After A Late-2024 Spending Splurge

On the inflation front, that last mile has proven to be the toughest. The Core PCE Price Index rose 2.6% from 12 months ago, according to the January report. That’s a steady rate since the middle of 2024. At the same time, forward breakeven inflation rates have jumped to nearly their highest levels since 2022.

Moreover, consumer surveys point to rising fears of imminent inflation increases. It’s critical that households’ view of inflation remains anchored to not create a wage-price spiral. The Fed is monitoring it closely.

US Inflation Rate Stalls Above the Fed's 2% Target

Overall, the first quarter of 2025 is now seen growing at a low 1.3% annualized rate, according to prediction markets.

Then on Friday, the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model stunned investors by printing –1.5%, though much of the decline is due to changes in trade (a surge in net imports weights on the GDP measure) and inventories.

To be fair, the NY Fed Q1 GDP nowcast points to domestic growth of 2.94% in the week-ending February 28, so prudence is required when deciphering these early quarter model outputs.

Tepid Q1 GDP Growth Priced In

Speaking of the Fed, the bond market now prices in a mid-year cut after an extended pause. Going out the next 12 months, 65 basis points of easing is discounted, which is an increase compared to a few weeks ago as growth concerns increase.

The FOMC meets next on March 19, and the Committee will pencil in their latest dot plots as part of the quarterly Summary of Economic Projections.

Fed Expected To Cut One or Two Times This Year

Amid much macro drama, S&P 500 companies continue delivering impressive bottom-line numbers. The Q4 EPS growth rate is seen at 18.2%, the best YOY jump since Q4 2021, according to FactSet.

As a result, CY 2024 SPX EPS is now forecast to be near $242 while the current year’s growth rate is expected to be 12.1%, though per-share earnings expectations have dipped in the last handful of months.

S&P 500 Earnings Estimates Falling For 2025

Finally, readers know I like to eye calendar trends or clues on where stocks may go in the near term. Seeking Alpha’s seasonality data show that March features a strong 2.16% median March climb, but the massive decline five years ago puts the average gain at just 0.3%.

So, there is a bullish bias here, but we’ve seen volatility occasionally show itself during the final month of the first quarter.

S&P 500 Seasonality: Usually Strong March-July Returns

The Bottom Line

US stocks fell in February, with heavy losses among growth equities and small caps. Foreign markets and fixed income rallied as the US dollar gave background. I’d be remiss not to call out bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) severe monthly plunge of almost 20% and gold’s jump to an all-time high just below the $3,000 per ounce mark.

Heading into March, the focus will continue to be on macro conditions and what the Tariff Man has up his sleeve.

data.title: Silver Prices Will Surge To Triple Digits If Investors Panic
data.description:
It has been a while since my last article about silver. When I last wrote about the market for this precious metal in October, there was a BRICS summit where economic and political issues were discussed. Gold, as well as silver, surged to multi-year highs as there were speculations that the BRICS members would start shifting away from the dollar. As I wrote in my previous article about the US economy, President Trump was highly worried that the alliance would create a common currency, which would allow them to move away from the USD as the medium of exchange in international trade. Since that hype, both gold and silver prices have corrected somewhat only to rise again. Yet, they are still trading off their all-time highs reached in October last year. In this article, I will write about silver's potential to rise further.

Silver prices—what has happened?

The graph below shows the recent two years of silver’s price gains. As you can see, a lion’s share of these price increases was made in the first half of the year 2024. As I have also mentioned above, silver touched multi-year highs in October last year during the BRICS+ summit in Russia’s Kazan.

But what are the reasons for the most recent rally? After President Trump’s inauguration at the end of January this year, Trump’s administration started introducing tariffs against several countries, including China. Why did silver react then? Silver, just like gold, is highly sensitive to economic and political threats. This means that investors rush to buy these safe-heaven metals to protect their savings. This also happened this time, it seems to me.

Why is silver worth investing in?

After all, it is not gold. It is in much greater supply than the yellow precious metal. Moreover, in many countries there has been a gold standard. This means that a particular currency’s value was tied to gold. For example, in the US before Nixon’s USD reform, one ounce of gold was used to trade for $35. In other words, the price was fixed, and gold was convertible into dollars at that price. After Nixon’s reform, gold’s supply remained unchanged, while the U.S. Federal Reserve was allowed to print more money to finance the ballooning national budget’s deficits. The gold price, therefore, was not tied to the dollar’s value and started surging as the USD supply was rising.

But what do gold prices have to do with silver? After all, silver standards have never been very popular, unlike gold standards. However, in the eyes of investors, silver has been considered to be a “small brother” of gold. In other words, it is considered a cheaper and more accessible alternative to gold and also as a hedge against inflation because the money supply often rises at a much faster pace than silver reserves.

Apart from being a safe-haven metal, silver is also an industrial metal used in green energy—most importantly, solar batteries—jewelry, the production of semiconductors and other electronics. So, what are the key factors determining the silver demand and therefore its prices?

The key factors behind silver’s demand

As I have mentioned in my other articles about silver, there are several factors influencing the demand for the shiny gray metal, namely monetary policies, political events, and industrial demand. Industrial demand is mostly linked to factors like green energy, electronics goods like smartphones and laptops, and even artificial intelligence ('AI'). In one of my previous articles about silver, I wrote about the way silver is useful in AI technologies and why it could get very popular.

However, inflation, other macroeconomic indicators, political events and trends, and general uncertainty also affect investors’ appetite for silver, more than do industrial demand trends. Most of the demand for silver, therefore, is speculative. In other words, if there is market panic due to the Fed’s policy shift—in other words, more policy easing than previously expected—unforeseen political events or many professional investors rushing to buy silver, then private investors are likely to follow suit.

Is silver really that expensive?

As you can see from the graphs above, silver appears to be really expensive. However, let us see the longer history graph of silver prices, adjusted for inflation.

So, as you can see from the diagram above, the current price of over $30 per ounce is quite average, neither too high nor particularly low. There were high peaks in 2011 and 1980 accordingly. Adjusted for today’s money, their prices would have been almost $70 in 2011 and just over $140 in 1980.

As you can see even from the graph unadjusted for inflation above, silver prices are still off their peaks.

But how about the relationship between silver quantity and dollar liquidity? Well, it is quite direct. As I have mentioned above, even though silver was never directly tied to the USD the way gold was, many investors have rushed to buy silver coins, bars, or cutlery to compensate for the fiat currency’s depreciation. The most obvious situation when it happened was in 1980, when the Hunts speculated with the prices for the precious metal and smaller investors followed their example. It is well possible that prices might even touch $140 per ounce in today's money if large investors show a significant interest in the gray, shiny metal.

Silver used to serve as a medium of exchange in earlier centuries. It was used to make coins that then played the role of money.

So, how about the dollar-to-silver ratio nowadays? It is currently standing at $1,163 for one ounce of silver, according to the US Debt Clock website. True, the five-year average is even higher than that, currently standing at $1,561 for one ounce.

But bear in mind that in dollar terms, silver has been trading for just over $30 per ounce for a while, far below the dollar-to-silver ratio, which suggests its substantial undervaluation.

My take on the silver market

So, what does it all mean for the silver market? In simple terms, it means that silver is still undervalued and has further potential to rise in value. It is not stored by central bankers the way gold is. However, it is one of investors’ refuges in case of an economic or political crisis or softer monetary policies of central bankers. There might be a short-lived correction after a prolonged rally only for the prices to rise even further, in my opinion. The only question that remains is how to invest. In other words, some investors might prefer to buy stocks of silver miners. There is a risk, however, that investors might not be well-informed about a given company’s accounting fundamentals. Some investors, in turn, might buy futures, options, and ETFs. Unfortunately, there is much more paper silver than physical silver available. The paper-to-silver ratio is a whopping 378.39 to 1, according to the US National Debt Clock website. So, despite high storage costs, I am personally more in favor of investing in physical silver. I find it possible that silver prices may rise to $140 per ounce if there is substantial investor panic and key market players start buying silver.
